sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Investigators compared nutrient absorption in intestinal segments exposed to normal bile, no bile salts, or a pancreatic lipase inhibitor. Fat absorption fell sharply when bile salts were absent or lipase was inhibited, whereas carbohydrate and protein absorption were only mildly affected. Microscopy showed normal villus structure in all groups, suggesting that the major effects were on digestion and micelle formation rather than loss of absorptive surface area.
questions.stem: Which process is most directly impaired when bile salts are absent?

choices.B: Emulsification and micelle formation for lipid absorption
choices.A: Peptide bond hydrolysis by pepsin in the stomach

explanations.B: Correct. Bile salts help emulsify lipids and form micelles, explaining the selective fall in fat absorption.
explanations.A: Pepsin acts in the stomach and is not the bile-dependent step.
explanations.C: Carbohydrate absorption is relatively preserved in the figure.
explanations.D: Protein absorption is only mildly affected, so amino acid transport is not the primary defect.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Nutrient absorption under digestive perturbations.

questions.stem: Why does pancreatic lipase inhibition reduce fat absorption even when bile salts are present?

choices.D: Triglycerides must be hydrolyzed into absorbable fatty acids and monoacylglycerols
choices.A: Lipase directly transports glucose across enterocytes
choices.B: Bile salts are proteins that require lipase for translation
choices.C: Lipase converts amino acids into chylomicrons

explanations.D: Correct. Pancreatic lipase digests triglycerides into products that can enter micelles and be absorbed.
explanations.B: Bile salts are amphipathic molecules, not proteins translated in the lumen.
explanations.C: Chylomicrons are assembled from lipids inside enterocytes, not amino acids converted by lipase.

questions.stem: Which structural feature increases intestinal absorptive capacity but was not responsible for the experimental differences?

choices.A: Villi and microvilli increasing surface area

explanations.A: Correct. The passage states villus structure was normal, so surface area was not the source of reduced fat absorption.

questions.stem: Fat absorbed by enterocytes is first packaged for lymphatic transport mainly as:

choices.B: HDL particles made in the stomach

explanations.C: Correct. Long-chain dietary lipids are re-esterified and packaged into chylomicrons that enter lacteals.
explanations.A: Glucose is transported in blood, not as albumin-bound fat cargo.
explanations.B: HDL participates in reverse cholesterol transport and is not made in the stomach as the main dietary lipid particle.
explanations.D: Glycogen is a glucose storage polymer, not a lipid transport particle.

questions.stem: Which result best supports the claim that the experiment targeted digestion rather than epithelial destruction?

choices.B: Normal villus structure in all experimental groups
choices.A: Lower fat absorption in two groups
choices.C: Presence of pancreatic enzymes in the intestinal lumen
choices.D: Different labels on the graph axes

explanations.B: Correct. Normal villus morphology argues against loss of absorptive surface and supports a functional digestive defect.
explanations.A: Lower fat absorption shows an effect but not whether it is structural or digestive.
explanations.C: Enzyme presence alone does not exclude epithelial injury.
explanations.D: Graph labels do not provide biological evidence.

questions.passage: After a mixed meal, subjects showed transient increases in plasma insulin and skeletal muscle glucose uptake, while glucagon decreased. In a separate trial, subjects received an insulin receptor antagonist before the meal. Plasma glucose rose higher than normal, and glycogen synthesis in liver and muscle decreased despite normal pancreatic insulin secretion.
questions.stem: Which normal post-meal change is shown in the figure?

choices.A: Increased insulin with increased muscle glucose uptake
choices.C: Complete suppression of blood glucose absorption
choices.D: Decreased insulin receptor signaling due to low glucose

explanations.A: Correct. After feeding, insulin rises and promotes glucose uptake into skeletal muscle, matching the figure.
explanations.B: Glucagon decreases after the meal in the figure.
explanations.C: Dietary glucose is absorbed; it is not completely suppressed.
explanations.D: High nutrient availability normally promotes, not decreases, insulin signaling.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Hormonal and tissue responses after a mixed meal.

questions.stem: The insulin receptor antagonist most directly blocks which type of receptor signaling?

explanations.D: Correct. The insulin receptor is a receptor tyrosine kinase that activates downstream pathways promoting glucose uptake and glycogen synthesis.
explanations.A: Insulin receptors are not ligand-gated chloride channels.
explanations.B: Insulin is peptide hormone and does not signal through intracellular steroid receptors.
explanations.C: Insulin signaling is not primarily GPCR-cAMP signaling.

questions.stem: Why did plasma glucose rise higher with the antagonist despite normal insulin secretion?

choices.C: Target tissues could not respond normally to insulin
choices.B: Glucagon could no longer bind hepatic receptors

explanations.C: Correct. An antagonist at the insulin receptor produces insulin resistance at target tissues even if secretion is normal.
explanations.A: The passage says insulin secretion was normal.
explanations.B: Blocked glucagon signaling would not explain decreased insulin-mediated uptake.
explanations.D: If absorption were blocked, plasma glucose would tend to rise less, not more.

questions.stem: Which transporter is most insulin-responsive in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue?

explanations.B: Correct. Insulin stimulates translocation of GLUT4 to the plasma membrane in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue.
explanations.A: SGLT1 is a sodium-glucose cotransporter in intestine and kidney.
explanations.D: Aquaporin-2 is regulated by ADH in collecting duct.

questions.stem: Which pathway would insulin normally stimulate after a meal?

choices.B: Glycogen breakdown in skeletal muscle for export as glucose
choices.C: Proteasomal degradation of insulin receptors as the main response

explanations.D: Correct. Insulin promotes anabolic storage processes such as glycogen synthesis after feeding.
explanations.A: Ketogenesis is favored during fasting, when insulin is low and glucagon is high.
explanations.B: Skeletal muscle lacks glucose-6-phosphatase and does not export free glucose as a major response.
explanations.C: Receptor degradation is not the main physiological post-meal response.
